metadata.title: Squeeze the position, the momentum spreads (uncertainty is just Fourier)

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to actually see the Gaussian saturate the bandwidth bound the way you claimed. I keep picturing delta-x times delta-k equals one half as some arbitrary threshold, but you said the Gaussian hits it exactly while other shapes fall short. Help me build the intuition: (1) show me why a square top-hat packet in space has a WIDER momentum spread than a Gaussian of the same position width; (2) walk through where the h-bar substitution enters to turn one half into h-bar over two; (3) give me one non-quantum example, like radar pulses, where this same bound bites.

props.command: I want to actually see the Gaussian saturate the bandwidth bound the way you claimed. I keep picturing delta-x times delta-k equals one half as some arbitrary threshold, but you said the Gaussian hits it exactly while other shapes fall short. Help me build the intuition: (1) show me why a square top-hat packet in space has a WIDER momentum spread than a Gaussian of the same position width; (2) walk through where the h-bar substitution enters to turn one half into h-bar over two; (3) give me one non-quantum example, like radar pulses, where this same bound bites.
props.segment: Squeeze the position, the momentum spreads

beats.narration_text: Heisenberg says you cannot know position and momentum at the same time. But this rule was already in mathematics — long before quantum mechanics.

card.dek: Squeeze the position, the momentum spreads

beats.narration_text: A Fourier transform predicts that pinching a wave in space must widen it in frequency. Multiply by h-bar and you get Heisenberg. So why is the uncertainty principle called quantum?

card.dek: delta-x times delta-k >= 1/2 (pure math). Multiply by h-bar: delta-x times delta-p >= h-bar/2. Why is this called quantum?

beats.narration_text: A wave packet is a sum of pure sine waves. Squeeze the packet into a narrow spike in space and you need many different frequencies to build it.
The wider you spread the packet in space, the fewer frequencies it needs. This is not physics — it is the mathematics of wave superposition.
The Fourier transform pairs position and wavenumber. A narrow Gaussian in x transforms to a wide Gaussian in k. They breathe in reciprocal proportion.
The bandwidth bound: delta-x times delta-k is at least one half. The Gaussian saturates this bound exactly — it is the most tightly packed wave shape possible.
In quantum mechanics, momentum is h-bar times wavenumber. That single substitution converts the math theorem into Heisenberg's inequality — no new physics required.
The uncertainty principle applies to sound, light, and radar — any wave system. Quantum mechanics inherits it by identifying momentum with wavenumber.

props.headline: The uncertainty principle applies to sound, light, and radar — any…

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: a Gaussian packet with position width 0.5 nanometers. The Fourier bound forces a momentum spread of at least h-bar over one nanometer. Halve the width — the momentum spread doubles.
Squeezing a wave in space widens it in frequency — that is the Fourier bandwidth bound. Multiply by h-bar and you have Heisenberg. The uncertainty is Fourier, not mystery.

card.dek: Fourier bandwidth: delta-x times delta-k >= 1/2. Multiply by h-bar: uncertainty principle.
